channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: In This Nazi-Era Restitution Dispute, the Focus Turns to a Missing Cow
item.description: A family is vying for the return of a painting it thought was by Rubens. But an expert says it’s a copy because it does not include the artist’s depiction of a peeing cow.
item.dc:creator: Catherine Hickley

item.title: The Dance Quotes and Callbacks in ‘Schmigadoon’ and ‘The Jellicle Ball’
item.description: Breaking down the choreographic allusions in “Schmigadoon” and “Cats: The Jellicle Ball,” two gotta-dance, Tony-nominated shows.
item.dc:creator: Brian Seibert

item.title: Cannes Film Festival Preview: 4 Story Lines to Watch for
item.description: The lineup isn’t very starry, but changes in the Oscar rules could affect the Palme d’Or choice. That’s one development we’re tracking.
item.dc:creator: Kyle Buchanan
item.media:credit: Guillaume Horcajuelo/Pool/Getty Images
item.media:description: Pedro Almodóvar at Cannes in 2023. He’s returning with “Bitter Christmas.”

item.title: Dudamel Unites N.Y. Philharmonic and Spanish Harlem Orchestra
item.description: Gustavo Dudamel brought the New York Philharmonic and the Spanish Harlem Orchestra together for the first time, at Lincoln Center and uptown.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney and James Estrin
item.category: Loew's 175th Street Theater/United Palace of Cultural Arts (Manhattan, NY)
item.media:description: Gustavo Dudamel congratulating the percussionist Luis Quintero, from the Spanish Harlem Orchestra, during a performance with the New York Philharmonic last week.

item.title: How Israel Turned Eurovision’s Stage Into a Soft Power Tool
item.description: Israel’s efforts to influence Eurovision’s vote were broader and started years earlier than previously known.
item.dc:creator: Mara Hvistendahl and Alex Marshall
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Denis Balibouse/Reuters

item.title: ‘The Comeback’ Knows What Scares Hollywood
item.description: The HBO comedy, which wrapped up on Sunday, often seemed to be about whatever was keeping Lisa Kudrow and her collaborators up at night. But Valerie Cherish remained undaunted.
item.dc:creator: James Poniewozik and Alissa Wilkinson
item.media:credit: Erin Simkin/HBO
item.media:description: Lance Barber, who played the obnoxious writer Paulie G. in the first season, briefly returned in the final season.

item.title: Lost Movie Music? On CD? La-La Land Is an Anomaly. (And a Success.)
item.description: The California record label scours movie studio archives and composers’ personal collections to locate music from older films or TV recordings.
item.dc:creator: Brian Raftery
item.media:credit: Alex Welsh for The New York Times
item.media:description: Matt Verboys (center), one of the founders of La-La Land Records, at work at the label’s offices in Burbank, Calif.

item.title: Kevin Hart Roast: Highlights From Tom Brady, the Rock, Katt Williams and More
item.description: Tom Brady, the Rock, Chelsea Handler and even Hart’s longtime rival Katt Williams told jokes at Hart’s expense at a Netflix roast.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams
item.media:credit: Kevin Mazur/Getty Images
item.media:description: Actor and comedian Kevin Hart sat through many jokes.

item.title: How Much Art Is Too Much? A Guide to the New York Fairs.
item.description: Latin American galleries. World-class photography. Upcycled design. Art fairs will take over New York this week. Here are seven (of many) worth a visit.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Sherman

item.title: London Rooftop Where Beatles Played Last Gig to Become Museum
item.description: The townhouse at 3 Savile Row, where the band also recorded “Let It Be,” will open to the public next year, the group’s record label said.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:credit: Apple Corps Ltd Images
item.media:description: The Beatles playing their final performance on the roof of 3 Savile Row in London in 1969.

item.title: On ‘S.N.L.’, Brett Kavanaugh, Kash Patel and Pete Hegseth Walk Into a Bar
item.description: Matt Damon and Aziz Ansari return to roles they have played on “Saturday Night Live” alongside Colin Jost, in an episode hosted by Damon.
item.dc:creator: Dave Itzkoff

item.title: Review: ‘The Receptionist’ Can’t Help You Today
item.description: A stale revival of Adam Bock’s cog-in-the-office-machine dark comedy lacks specificity and bite.
item.dc:creator: Brittani Samuel
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: Katie Finneran, left, and Mallori Johnson in “The Receptionist” at The Pershing Square Signature Center in Manhattan.

item.title: Taj Mahal’s New Album, ‘Time,’ Keeps the Blues Looking Ahead
item.description: Rooted in the blues and connected worldwide, the musician, 83, is still looking ahead.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles and Daniel Weiss
item.media:description: “Jazz will give you back your mind, reggae will give you back your body, but the blues will give you back your soul. That’s why they ain’t goin’ nowhere.”

item.title: ‘The Lost Boys’ Helped Define the ’80s in More Ways Than One
item.description: The film that’s been adapted into a new musical created a memorable aesthetic with its cast and outfits. Its ideas about the nuclear family also made it a movie for its time.
item.dc:creator: Maya Phillips
item.media:credit: Warner Bros.
item.media:description: From left, Billy Wirth, Kiefer Sutherland, Brooke McCarter and Alex Winter in “The Lost Boys.”

item.title: A Reporter Shares His Secrets to Covering the Cannes Film Festival
item.description: Kyle Buchanan, who has attended the annual launchpad for Oscar contenders in France since 2013, subsists on Red Bull and around four hours of sleep.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Antonin Thuillier/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The red carpet at the 2025 Cannes Film Festival. Many movies screened during the festival go on to earn Academy Award nominations.

item.title: Stephen Colbert Hosts 4 of His ‘Best Television Friends’
item.description: Jimmy Kimmel, Jimmy Fallon, John Oliver and Seth Meyers, co-hosts of the Strike Force Five podcast along with Colbert, joined as guests on what is likely their last appearance together on the “Late Show.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)

item.title: At Frieze New York and Beyond, Indigenous Artists Are in the Spotlight
item.description: With works showing in New York, Venice and elsewhere, Sara Flores, Suzanne Kite and Seba Calfuqueo are exploding past notions of what, exactly, Indigenous art is.
item.dc:creator: Nazanin Lankarani

item.title: Amitav Ghosh Brings the Main Character of ‘Ghost Eye’ to Life, With the Help of a Sketch Artist
item.description: With the help of a forensic artist, Amitav Ghosh puts a face to the name of Varsha Gupta, the central figure of his new novel, “Ghost Eye.”
item.dc:creator: Jenny Comita

item.title: ‘Off-Campus,’ and 8 Things to Watch on TV This Week
item.description: A TV adaptation of a new hockey romance series. And a comedy series starring two “One Tree Hill” alums finds a new home.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.media:credit: Courtesy of Prime
item.media:description: From left: Antonio Cipriano, Stephen Kalyn, Jalen Thomas Brooks and Belmont Cameli in “Off-Campus.”

item.title: ‘The Comeback’ Season 3 Finale Recap: All’s Well?
item.description: Valerie makes the case that her tale is one of survival, not humiliation. “You’ve been telling the wrong story,” she tells Jane.
item.dc:creator: Noel Murray
item.media:credit: Erin Simkin/HBO
item.media:description: Despite the tendency for some people around Valerie (Lisa Kudrow) to view her as a doormat, she has always seen every painful moment as a step forward.

item.title: Maya Lin Connects Nature to a New Manhattan Skyscraper and Beyond
item.description: The famed artist and designer drew on her lifelong love of the environment for inspiration for new work in New York and Chicago.
item.dc:creator: Jane L. Levere
item.media:credit: Shuran Huang for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lin’s new work at the JP Morgan Chase building in New York, a sculpture composed on massive gray stone walls, and the adjoining plaza were envisioned in part as a respite from the cacophony of the city.

item.title: New Books Provide Divergent Views of the Art Market
item.description: Three new books — a sweeping work of nonfiction, a cheeky memoir and a dual biography — provide divergent views on the business of buying and selling, and they are out just in time for New York Art Week.
item.dc:creator: Ted Loos

item.title: Michael Joo Steps Into the Art World’s Spotlight
item.description: Michael Joo is appearing in multiple shows, from New York and the San Francisco Bay Area to a Mediterranean island and Venice.
item.dc:creator: Ted Loos
item.media:credit: Oliver Farshi for The New York Times

item.title: A Monument to Chocolate Is Wrapped in Layers of Mexican History
item.description: Combining a colonial house and a contemporary addition, the new Museum of Cacao & Chocolate sits on a grisly Aztec souvenir.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Gallow
item.media:credit: Luis Antonio Rojas for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Huei, or great Tzompantli. It displays hundreds of human skulls belonging to people who were believed to have been sacrificed in the 15th century.

item.title: If You See Only 6 Venice Shows, Make It These
item.description: Thought-provoking and senses-stirring works on war, art patronage, and the Black experience in America are just some of our critics’ picks.
item.dc:creator: Jason Farago, Blake Gopnik and Aruna D’Souza
item.media:credit: Casey Kelbaugh for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ashfika Rahman’s “Than Para — No Land Without Us” (2025-ongoing) in the “Still Joy” group exhibition at the Pinchuk Art Center.

item.title: Rocky Has Entered the Building
item.description: The statue has long drawn fans to the steps of the Philadelphia Museum of Art, but now it’s inside, anchoring an exhibition that investigates race, activism and violence.
item.dc:creator: Christopher Hawthorne

item.title: These Artisans, Showing at TEFAF New York, Push the Limits of Materials
item.description: A couple grow their own furniture. Another artist polishes metal to a mirror sheen. A third collaborates with insects. Together, they are redefining craft.
item.dc:creator: Dale Berning Sawa

item.title: A Traveling Bookstore Keeps on Rolling
item.description: Rita Collins had a dream for her retirement: bringing books and people together all over the country. Behind the wheel of a van she’s making it happen.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Fremson
item.media:credit: Ruth Fremson/The New York Times

item.title: Hugh Jackman in a Truly PG-Rated Murder Mystery
item.description: Our movie critic Alissa Wilkinson reviews “The Sheep Detectives” and asks: Why aren’t more movies like it?
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson, Gabriel Blanco, Edward Vega, Haimy Assefa and Laura Salaberry

item.title: Charli XCX, Rolling Stones: 8 Songs We’re Talking About This Week
item.description: Charli XCX rediscovers rock, while Beatles and Rolling Stones keep making it.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:credit: Scott A Garfitt/Invision, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Charli XCX says she’s “on to the next” on her new single, “Rock Music.”

item.title: When Mark Consuelos Wants to Escape New York, He Goes Here
item.description: “The midcentury architecture is fascinating. It’s also a place where I’m outside almost every waking hour of the day.”
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.category: Live With Kelly and Mark (TV Program)
item.media:credit: John Lamparski/Getty Images

item.title: Dynamic Artistic Duos are on Display in New York This Spring
item.description: Three exhibitions are pairing artists to reveal how their works complement — or contrast with — each other.
item.dc:creator: Ted Loos

item.title: The Korean Artist Park Daesung Is In the Spotlight at 81
item.description: The Korean artist is nearly 81, but his works have been featured in shows in Chicago, London, Washington and Los Angeles. Now they are also headed to TEFAF New York.
item.dc:creator: David Belcher
item.media:credit: Jun Michael Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Park Daesung at his studio in Gyeongju, South Korea. Gana Art, a major gallery in Seoul, will take four of his paintings to TEFAF New York.

item.title: Blake Lively Settlement Had No Financial Payout, Filing Confirms
item.description: Justin Baldoni’s company did not agree to make a payout as part of the deal that ended Ms. Lively’s lawsuit, but she is able to seek compensation through a separate legal avenue.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.media:credit: Seth Wenig/Associated Press
item.media:description: As the legal dispute between Justin Baldoni and Blake Lively continues, a new court filing confirms their settlement agreement includes no financial payout to her. But it enables her to seek compensation through an alternative legal avenue.

item.title: An Artist Who Sees the Beauty in Dust
item.description: Reggie Burrows Hodges discusses Malta, Caravaggio and a painting inspired by the winds of the Sahara.
item.dc:creator: Gazelle Mba

item.title: Edith Eva Eger, Psychologist Who Barely Survived Auschwitz, Dies at 98
item.description: Her time in concentration camps brought her an understanding of humanity that helped her treat her patients.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir

item.title: J.H. Prynne, Erudite and Elusive British Poet, Dies at 89
item.description: Even admirers admitted his densely intellectual work could be “punishing.” Still, some considered him one of England’s most important poets.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams
item.media:credit: Chris Bourchier/Shutterstock
item.media:description: J.H. Prynne in a rare photograph in 2004. A notoriously dense and intellectual poet, he disclosed virtually nothing of his personal life and refused to pose for the camera, even for his book jackets.

item.title: David Attenborough, a Voice of Nature, Turns 100
item.description: Pictures and striking scenes from the making of perhaps the world’s most celebrated naturalist.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe
item.media:credit: John Stillwell/Press Association, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Mr. Attenborough enrolled at the University of Cambridge in 1945, where he studied natural sciences, including geology and zoology.

item.title: Review: At City Ballet, Tiler Peck Shows Shimmering Restraint
item.description: The star ballerina is also an accomplished choreographer. Her sophomore work for City Ballet is bigger and better than her first.
item.dc:creator: Brian Seibert
item.media:credit: Rachel Papo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Roman Mejia, center, in the premiere of Tiler Peck’s “Symphonie Espagnole” on Thursday.

item.title: Five Science Fiction Movies to Stream Now
item.description: Among this month’s picks: a zombie road movie, an alien action movie and Gerard Butler in a postapocalyptic sequel.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli

item.title: Paul Klee’s ‘Angelus Novus’ Joins Show at Jewish Museum in New York
item.description: “Angelus Novus,” an artwork with a fascinating back story, including most recently a wartime delay, is a late addition to an already impressive Klee survey at the Jewish Museum.
item.dc:creator: Deborah Solomon

item.title: Venice Biennale: Austria, Japan, Belgium and Other Big Hits
item.description: A doll-focused show, a contemplative garden, a sizzling hothouse: It’s all to see at the world’s top contemporary art event.
item.dc:creator: Jason Farago, Alex Marshall, Laura Rysman, Aruna D’Souza and Julia Halperin
item.media:credit: Casey Kelbaugh for The New York Times
item.media:description: In the pavilion, performers scurry up and down wooden racks that hold plaster tablets with words in various languages embossed on them.

item.title: Artists’ Strike Closes Pavilions at Venice Biennale, Adding to Upheaval
item.description: The hottest exhibitions at the world’s major art exhibition were shuttered on Friday as part of a pro-Palestinian demonstration.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:credit: Casey Kelbaugh for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Netherlands’ shuttered Venice Biennale pavilion displayed a flag and fliers in support of Palestinians on Friday.

item.title: Without Its Expert Jury, Venice Biennale Lets the Public Choose Its Prize Winners
item.description: The jury resigned last week, so the international art event is letting viewers pick the best artist from the main exhibition and the national pavilions.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small
item.media:credit: Casey Kelbaugh for The New York Times
item.media:description: A crowd of visitors at the Giardini in Venice during the Biennale preview days on Thursday.

item.title: ‘Amadeus’ Review: Mozart vs. Salieri, Round 3
item.description: The story of genius and jealousy, a major award winner onstage and on film, returns as a TV series starring Will Sharpe and Paul Bettany.
item.dc:creator: Mike Hale
item.media:credit: Starz
item.media:description: Paul Bettany plays Antonio Salieri in a new series version of “Amadeus,” on Starz.

item.title: How ‘The Sheep Detectives’ Brought its Ovine Sleuths to Life
item.description: The filmmakers behind this adaptation of a best-selling novel were adamant that their ovine sleuths not seem like humans in, well, sheep’s clothing.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Lyall

item.title: A Very American Controversy at the Venicle Biennale
item.description: Trump has taken an active role in the arts in his second term, which may be evident in the work on display at the Venice Biennale — depending on how you look at it.
item.dc:creator: M.H. Miller
item.media:credit: Andrea Frazzetta for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jeffrey Uslip with a sculpture by Alma Allen at the U.S. Pavilion.

item.title: 5 New Movies Our Critics Are Talking About This Week
item.description: Whether you’re a casual moviegoer or an avid buff, our reviewers think these films are worth knowing about.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: The Hopelessness of ‘The Audacity’
item.description: In AMC’s merciless satire, the tech lords are extracting all they can from the business (and us) before everything goes to hell.
item.dc:creator: James Poniewozik

item.title: After a Fire, a Director Finds a Lifeline in ‘Remarkably Bright Creatures’
item.description: Olivia Newman lost her home in the 2025 California wildfires. Telling the story of a woman who connects with an octopus after a major loss proved a balm.
item.dc:creator: Nicole Sperling
item.media:credit: Chantal Anderson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Olivia Newman with her dog Pepper on the lot in Altadena, Calif., where her house once stood.

item.title: ‘The Python Hunt’ Review: A Real Contest as Riveting as Fiction
item.description: The Florida Python Challenge brings out a fascinating cast of characters, all captured by the director Xander Robin’s camera.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: Why Are So Many Celebrities Co-Producing On Broadway?
item.description: Barack Obama, Kim Kardashian and Bowen Yang have all signed on as co-producers of Broadway shows. Our theater reporter Michael Paulson explains why.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson, Haimy Assefa, Gabriel Blanco and Laura Salaberry

item.title: 5 Minutes That Will Make You Love Ron Carter
item.description: Christian McBride, Endea Owens, Large Professor and more writers and musicians share favorite tracks from the bass maestro, who turned 89 this week.
item.dc:creator: Giovanni Russonello

item.title: Matthew Wong’s Grieving Mother Protects His Artistic Legacy
item.description: The troubled painter Matthew Wong’s star was on the rise when he died at 35. His mother, Monita Wong, is making sure his work is seen, especially in Venice.
item.dc:creator: Arthur Lubow
item.media:credit: Alana Paterson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Monita Wong holds a mask that her son, the artist Matthew Wong, who died by suicide in 2019, made in the fourth grade in Hong Kong.

item.title: Why Is Everyone Obsessed With Bogs?
item.description: From fashion to art, an explainer on our love of wetlands.
item.dc:creator: Zoey Poll

item.title: Billie Eilish and James Cameron on ‘Hit Me Hard and Soft: The Tour’
item.description: The musician and director teamed up to bring Hit Me Hard and Soft: The Tour — with poignant moments onstage and off — to theaters.
item.dc:creator: Steve Knopper
item.category: Billie Eilish -- Hit Me Hard and Soft: The Tour (Movie)

item.title: After 43 Years, an Unproduced Larry David Script Surfaces Online
item.description: “Prognosis: Negative” was too dark for its time, but its title became a running “Seinfeld” joke and its premise set the table for “Curb Your Enthusiasm.”
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.media:credit: Ralph Dominguez/MediaPunch, via Associated Press

item.title: Historical Fiction Books That Illustrate the Bonds Between Mother and Child
item.description: The best-selling author Stephanie Dray recommends books that explore the bonds between mothers and their children across centuries.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Dray
item.media:credit: Image by The New York Times; Author photo by Kelsey Edwards

item.title: Late Night Recaps Rubio’s Peacemaking Visit to Pope Leo
item.description: Jimmy Fallon joked that President Trump and Pope Leo are putting Marco Rubio, who is Catholic, “in the middle and seeing which parent he goes to.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)

item.title: Philip Caputo, Who Wrote Blistering Vietnam War Memoir, Dies at 84
item.description: “A Rumor of War,” about his service as a Marine Corps infantry officer and published in 1977, relentlessly detailed “the things men do in war and the things war does to them.”
item.dc:creator: Joseph Berger
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Frank Lennon/Toronto Star, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Philip Caputo in 1987. “To call it the best book about Vietnam is to trivialize it,” the novelist and screenwriter John Gregory Dunne wrote in his review of “A Rumor of War.” “Heartbreaking, terrifying and enraging, it belongs to the literature of men at arms.”

item.title: DOGE’s Termination of Humanities Grants Is Ruled Unconstitutional
item.description: A lawsuit against the National Endowment for the Humanities drew wide attention for revealing how DOGE had used ChatGPT to cancel grants.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Schuessler
item.media:credit: Kayla Bartkowski/Getty Images
item.media:description: The National Endowment for the Humanities, created in 1965, supports scholarship, historical sites and public projects across the country.

item.title: ‘Remarkably Bright Creatures’ Review: Their Octopus Teacher
item.description: Sally Field and Lewis Pullman get help from an eight-tentacled friend in this bland adaptation of a hit novel.
item.dc:creator: Ben Kenigsberg
